name: Connecticut
notes_normalized: Cannabis is legal in Connecticut under state law for recreational adult use. Medical cannabis is also legal under a regulated state program in Connecticut. Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States. Possession is limited to varies (see official sources).
notes_raw: style="background:#196E92;" | {{Hs|1}} Cannabis in Connecticut is legal for recreational use since July 1, 2021. In June 2011, Governor Dannel Malloy signed legislation that decriminalized cannabis possession. Medical cannabis (2012). In June 2012, Governor Malloy signed into law a medical marijuana program for his state, following a 21–13 vote in the Senate. Recreational cannabis (2021). In April 2018, a recreational marijuana bill was approved to be sent to the General Assembly in a 27–24 vote. Efforts to legalize recreational marijuana renewed in 2021, with Lamont vowing to make a push for legalization that would enable sales to begin in May 2022. On June 22, 2021, Governor Lamont signed Senate Bill 1201 that legalized recreational cannabis. Medical use was legalized through legislation passed in 2012. In June 2011, Governor Dannel Malloy signed legislation that decriminalized cannabis possession. In June 2012, Governor Malloy signed into law a medical marijuana program for his state, following a 21–13 vote in the Senate. Efforts to legalize recreational marijuana renewed in 2021, with Lamont vowing to make a push for legalization that would enable sales to begin in May 2022. A bill by Lamont narrowly passed the state Judiciary Committee by a 22–16 vote on April 6, and another competing bill, praised by the legislature's progressives, had cleared the legislature's Labor and Public Employees Committee in a 9–4 vote just days earlier on March 25. On April 14, several Democratic legislators, including president pro tempore Martin Looney, said they would vote against the measure passed by the Judiciary, stating they were concerned about a lack of provisions for social equity within the bill. On June 22, 2021, Governor Lamont signed Senate Bill 1201 that legalized recreational cannabis. In June 2011, Governor Dannel Malloy signed legislation that decriminalized cannabis possession. The sale, manufacture, and cultivation of cannabis (aside from home grow) requires a license from the state. Products that contain delta-8-THC, delta-9-THC, or delta-10-THC are considered cannabis and may only be sold by licensed retailers. Individuals may not gift cannabis to another individual who has “paid” or “donated” for another product. The sale, manufacture, and cultivation of cannabis (aside from home grow) requires a license from the state. All adults age 21 and over will be permitted to grow six cannabis plants indoors within their homes beginning July 1, 2023. Outdoor cannabis cultivation remains a felony. On April 14, several Democratic legislators, including president pro tempore Martin Looney, said they would vote against the measure passed by the Judiciary, stating they were concerned about a lack of provisions for social equity within the bill.

state_modifiers.federal_conflict: Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States.
